content:
# Acts Chapter 12 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 239-240 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_012_page_001.jpg, ACT_012_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now about that time Herod the king put-forth the hands to-mistreat some of-those from the assembly.

**2** And he-killed James the brother of-John [with a] sword.

**3** And seeing that it-is pleasing to-the Jews, he-proceeded to-seize also Peter -- and they-were the days of-the unleavened-bread --

**4** whom also having-arrested he-put in prison, having-delivered [him] to-four quaternions of-soldiers to-guard him, intending after the Passover to-bring-him-up to-the people.

**5** So indeed Peter was-being-kept in the prison; but prayer was earnestly being-made by the assembly to God concerning him.

**6** And when Herod was-about to-bring-him-forth, in-that night Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, having-been-bound [with] two chains, and guards before the door were-keeping the prison.

**7** And behold, an angel of-[the] Lord stood-by, and a light shone in the cell; and having-struck the side of-Peter he-raised him saying, "Rise up in quickness." And the chains fell-off from the hands of-him.

**8** And the angel said to him, "Gird-yourself and bind-on the sandals of-you." And he-did so. And he-says to-him, "Throw-around the garment of-you and follow me."

**9** And having-gone-out he-was-following, and he-did not know that the-[thing] being-done through the angel is true, but he-was-thinking he-is-seeing a vision.

**10** And having-passed-through [the] first guard and [the] second, they-came to the iron gate, the-[one] leading into the city, which of-its-own-accord opened to-them; and having-gone-out they-went-forward one street, and immediately the angel departed from him.

**11** And Peter, having-come to himself, said, "Now I-know truly that [the] Lord sent-forth the angel of-him and rescued me out-of [the] hand of-Herod and all the expectation of-the people of-the Jews."

**12** And having-considered, he-came to the house of-Mary the mother of-John, the-[one] being-called Mark, where many were having-been-gathered-together and praying.

**13** And [when] he-knocked [at] the door of-the gate, a servant-girl came-near to-answer, by-name Rhoda.

**14** And having-recognized the voice of-Peter, from the joy she-did not open the gate, but having-run-in she-reported Peter to-be-standing before the gate.

**15** But they said to her, "You-are-mad." But she kept-insisting it-to-be so. And they were-saying, "It-is the angel of-him."

**16** But Peter was-continuing knocking; and having-opened they-saw him and were-amazed.

**17** And having-motioned to-them [with] the hand to-be-silent, he-related to-them how the Lord brought him out-of the prison. And he-said, "Report these-things to-James and to-the brothers." And having-gone-out he-went to another place.

**18** And [when] day came, there-was no small disturbance among the soldiers, [as to] what then [had] become of-Peter.

**19** And Herod having-sought him and not having-found, having-examined the guards, he-commanded [them] to-be-led-away; and having-gone-down from Judea to Caesarea he-was-staying.

**20** Now he-was furiously-angry with [the people of] Tyre and Sidon; but with-one-accord they-came to him, and having-persuaded Blastus, the-[one] over the bed-chamber of-the king, they-were-asking [for] peace, because of the being-nourished of-them the country from the royal-[land].

**21** And on an appointed day, Herod having-clothed-himself [in] royal garments and having-sat upon the judgment-seat, was-delivering-a-public-address to them.

**22** And the people were-crying-out, "[The] voice of-a-god and not of-a-man!"

**23** And immediately an angel of-[the] Lord struck him, because he-did not give the glory to-God; and having-become worm-eaten he-expired.

**24** But the word of-God was-growing and was-being-multiplied.

**25** And Barnabas and Saul returned out-of Jerusalem, having-fulfilled the ministry, having-taken-along also John, the-[one] being-called Mark.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **12:1 -- "put-forth the hands" (epebalen tas cheiras):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΠΕΒΑΛΕΝ ΤΑΣ ΧΕΙΡΑΣ, a Semitic idiom meaning "laid hands upon" in the sense of violent seizure. Translated literally to preserve the idiom.

2. **12:3 -- "he-proceeded" (prosetheto):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΟΣΕΘΕΤΟ, literally "he added" -- a Hebraism (Hebrew: vayosef) meaning "he did again" or "he proceeded further." This Semitic construction (prosithenai + infinitive) reflects Luke's deliberate imitation of Septuagintal style.

3. **12:4 -- "quaternions" (tetradion):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΕΤΡΑΔΙΟΙΣ -- groups of four soldiers each, totaling sixteen guards for rotating watch through four watches of the night.

4. **12:5 -- "earnestly" (ektenos):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΤΕΝΩΣ, meaning "fervently" or "without ceasing." Some manuscripts read ΕΚΤΕΝΗΣ (adjective form). Sinaiticus supports the adverbial form.

5. **12:7 -- "in quickness" (en tachei):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΑΧΕΙ. Preserved literally rather than smoothing to "quickly."

6. **12:10 -- "of-its-own-accord" (automate):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΥΤΟΜΑΤΗ -- the gate opened by itself, the same word from which English "automatic" derives.

7. **12:15 -- "the angel of-him":** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΑΓΓΕΛΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ. This reflects an early Jewish belief that each person has a guardian angel who can appear in their likeness.

8. **12:17 -- "to-James":** This is James the brother of the Lord (not the James killed in v. 2), who became the leader of the Jerusalem church. Sinaiticus names him without further identification, assuming the reader understands.

9. **12:19 -- "to-be-led-away" (apachthēnai):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΑΧΘΗΝΑΙ. This is a euphemism; the term means "led away to execution," the standard fate for guards who lost a prisoner under Roman military law.

10. **12:20 -- "furiously-angry" (thumomachon):** Sinaiticus reads ΘΥΜΟΜΑΧΩΝ -- a compound of thumos (fury) and machomai (to fight), indicating a hostile disposition rather than open warfare.

11. **12:23 -- "worm-eaten" (skolekobrotos):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΚΩΛΗΚΟΒΡΩΤΟΣ -- a compound of skolex (worm) and bibrosko (to eat). Josephus (Antiquities 19.8.2) provides a parallel account of Herod Agrippa I's death involving severe abdominal pain.

12. **12:25 -- "out-of Jerusalem" (ex Ierousalem):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΞ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ. A significant textual variant exists here: some manuscripts read ΕΙΣ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ ("to Jerusalem"), and others read ΑΠΟ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ ("from Jerusalem"). The Sinaiticus reading ΕΞ ("out of") supports the sense that Barnabas and Saul returned from Jerusalem to Antioch after delivering the famine relief (cf. 11:29-30).

13. **12:25 -- "having-taken-along" (symparalabontes):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΜΠΑΡΑΛΑΒΟΝΤΕΣ -- a compound participle meaning "having taken along with [them]," indicating Mark joined them on their return journey to Antioch, setting the stage for the first missionary journey (13:5).

-- EMET
